fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to build out the random forest engine we've been talking about. The core idea is that a developer should be able to point it at a dataset, say which column is the outcome they care about, and just get a trained model back without doing a bunch of manual setup work. We want to support the usual stuff — classifying categories, predicting numbers, and also that survival analysis thing we discussed (like what we did in the clinical data project last quarter, similar Surv() syntax). There should be a way to describe the problem either with a formula string or by just naming the column directly, both should work the same way. Data can come in as a typed table or as the all-numbers version of the same data, and the engine shouldn't care which one it gets. We also need proper error handling — not just crashes, but meaningful category labels for things like bad sampling settings, wrong split rules for the task type, missing values showing up, that kind of thing. Reproducibility is important so make sure seeding works correctly. We want OOB error, variable importance when asked for, confusion matrix for classification, probability outputs, per-tree prediction shapes, and survival timepoint tracking. Oh and warnings when something like unused categories get dropped during training. The adapter layer should be thin and separate from the actual engine logic — keep I/O concerns out of the core.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: fit(data_source: DataSource, spec: ProblemSpec, options: TrainOptions) -> TrainResult

trigger_keywords: how does the formula parser handle wrapped response columns
what happens when the left-hand side of the formula has a function call
does transform(Species) ~ . work the same as Species ~ .
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A formula whose left-hand side wraps the response column in any user-supplied function (e.g. transform(Species) ~ .) must still resolve correctly. The parser must extract the inner column name, look it up in the data source, and infer the task from that column's type. The predictor set is resolved identically to the unwrapped form — every other column in table order. The wrapper function name is ignored for task inference and predictor resolution purposes.

trigger_keywords: how is the survival formula written
what syntax is used to specify a time and event column together
how do I indicate a right-censored response in the formula
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The survival formula uses the syntax Surv(time_col, status_col) ~ . on the left-hand side. Both the time column and the status (event indicator) column are consumed by the response specification and are excluded from the predictor list. The dot on the right-hand side means every remaining column after removing both named columns. The inferred task is 'survival'.

trigger_keywords: what error is raised when sampling fraction is zero or negative
what happens if I pass sample_fraction=0
what is the valid range for the sampling fraction parameter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A scalar sampling fraction must lie in the half-open interval (0, 1]. A value of exactly 0, any negative value, or any value greater than 1 is rejected before training begins. The typed error category emitted is 'sample_fraction_out_of_range'. No partial training occurs.

trigger_keywords: can I pass a list of per-class sampling fractions for regression
what error occurs when a vector sampling fraction is used with a non-classification task
is per-class sampling supported for survival or regression
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A vector (per-class) sampling fraction is only valid when the inferred task is classification. Supplying a vector fraction for regression, survival, or probability tasks is rejected with the error category 'sample_fraction_vector_requires_classification'. The vector length must also exactly equal the number of response classes; a mismatch is rejected with 'sample_fraction_size_mismatch' and reports both the expected count and the provided count.

trigger_keywords: what happens when all per-class fractions are zero
what error is raised if the sampling fraction vector sums to zero
is a zero-sum fraction vector caught before training
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a per-class sampling fraction vector is provided and all values are zero (sum equals zero), training is rejected before any trees are grown. The typed error category is 'sample_fraction_sum_zero'.

trigger_keywords: what error occurs when sampling without replacement requests more samples than a class has
insufficient samples error format
how does the engine report that a class does not have enough rows for the requested fraction
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When sampling without replacement (replace=false) and the requested sample count for any class exceeds the number of available rows in that class, training is aborted. The error category is 'insufficient_samples_in_class'. Three additional fields are reported: 'class' (the name of the offending class), 'available' (the row count in that class), and 'requested' (the count that was requested). The class reported is the first class encountered that fails this check.

trigger_keywords: what error is raised when a splitting rule is used on the wrong task
can I use the variance split rule on a classification problem
how does the engine handle splitrule and task mismatches
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each splitting rule is tied to a specific task family. Using a regression-only rule (e.g. 'variance') on classification data emits error category 'splitrule_requires_regression' and also reports the 'splitrule' value that was supplied. Using a survival-only rule on classification data similarly emits a task-mismatch error category. No cross-family use of splitting rules is permitted.

trigger_keywords: how are missing values in predictor columns reported
what error is raised for NA values in training data
does the engine distinguish between missing response values and missing predictor values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Missing values in any predictor column used during training are rejected before fitting begins, with error category 'missing_data_in_predictors' and a 'columns' field listing the offending column names. Missing values in the response column are rejected with a distinct error category. At prediction time, missing values in predictor columns of the new data also emit 'missing_data_in_predictors' with 'columns' listing all affected columns in their original column order.

trigger_keywords: what is the shape of per-tree predictions for probability estimation
how many dimensions does the all-tree probability output have
what does predictions_shape mean for probability with predict=all
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a probability estimation task, requesting per-tree predictions (predict='all') returns a three-dimensional array. Its shape is reported as 'observations x classes x trees' (e.g. 150x3x5 for 150 rows, 3 classes, 5 trees). Classification and regression per-tree outputs are two-dimensional (observations x trees). Survival per-tree outputs are also three-dimensional but reported as two separate arrays — survival_shape and chf_shape — each of shape observations x event-times x trees.

trigger_keywords: how many unique event times does the survival model report
what are the evaluation timepoints for a survival forest
does the timepoints count include censored observations or only events
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A survival forest's evaluation timepoints are the sorted distinct event times observed in the training data — that is, the unique values of the time column where the status indicator equals 1 (event occurred), not the censored times. For the veteran dataset this yields 101 distinct event times. The timepoints are available via the model's timepoint accessor and are verified by comparing against the sorted unique event-time values extracted directly from the training time column filtered by status=1.

trigger_keywords: what warning is issued when training omits some response classes
what happens if the training subset does not contain all class levels
are predictions still valid when some response classes are absent from training rows
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the training data subset omits one or more response class levels that exist in the full dataset, a warning (not an error) is emitted with category 'dropped_unused_response_levels' and a 'levels' field listing the dropped class names. Predictions still span the full original set of classes (the prediction_levels field lists all original classes). For probability estimation the surviving class count is used. The model continues to train on the available classes.

trigger_keywords: what error is raised when variable importance is queried but was not enabled at training time
how do I get importance scores and what happens if I forget to enable them
what is the error category for missing importance
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If variable importance is queried on a model that was trained without importance scoring enabled, the engine emits error category 'importance_not_available'. Importance scoring must be explicitly requested at training time (e.g. importance='impurity'). When properly enabled, the engine returns one numeric importance value per predictor, keyed by predictor name, in predictor order.

trigger_keywords: what error occurs when survival timepoints are queried on a classification model
can I call the timepoints accessor on a regression forest
what is the error category for querying survival outputs on the wrong task
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Querying survival-specific outputs (such as timepoints, cumulative hazard, or survival function) on a model whose inferred task is not survival emits error category 'not_a_survival_forest'. This check applies to any accessor that is specific to the survival task family.

trigger_keywords: what happens when the mtry function raises an exception during training
how is an error inside the predictor count selection function reported
what error category covers failures in the custom mtry callable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the user-supplied function for selecting the number of predictors to consider at each split (mtry function) raises or returns an unusable value during evaluation, training is aborted and the error category 'mtry_function_error' is emitted. No partial model is returned.

trigger_keywords: what error is raised when a survival formula leaves no predictor columns
what happens if the only columns in the dataset are the time and status columns
how does the engine handle a survival problem with zero covariates
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If, after removing the time and status columns from the column set, no predictor columns remain, the specification is rejected before training. The typed error category is 'no_covariates'. This applies regardless of whether the problem was specified via formula or named columns.

trigger_keywords: what error is raised for an unknown predict type string
what happens if I pass an unsupported value to predict_type
is there a validation step for the prediction output type parameter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Requesting a prediction output type that the engine does not recognise (e.g. 'class' instead of a supported type string) is rejected with error category 'invalid_prediction_type'. This is validated before any prediction computation is performed.

trigger_keywords: how does the matrix data source determine the task for classification
when using an all-numeric matrix how does the engine know the response is categorical
does the matrix source need an explicit task declaration
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An all-numeric matrix source cannot infer a categorical task from column types alone because all columns are numeric. When a matrix-backed source is used for a classification or probability problem, the task must be declared explicitly in the specification (e.g. via a classification_matrix flag or an explicit task parameter). For regression and survival the numeric types are sufficient for inference. Predictor resolution on a matrix source selects every non-response column in table order, identical to a typed table source.

trigger_keywords: what does the confusion matrix output look like
what fields are reported for the confusion matrix operation
how are row totals verified in the confusion matrix
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The confusion matrix for a classification design is a square matrix with rows representing true classes and columns representing predicted classes. The output reports: 'dims' as RxC (e.g. 3x3), 'true_classes' listing the row class labels in order, 'predicted_classes' listing the column class labels in order, and 'row_totals' giving the count of training observations per true class. For the iris dataset with Species as response, row_totals must be 50,50,50 matching the 50 rows per class.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'error', 'warning', 'fit', 'equivalence', 'determinism', 'predict', 'confusion', 'probabilities', 'death_times', 'importance'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that survival analysis thing we discussed, similar Surv() syntax
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The survival formula syntax is Surv(time_col, status_col) ~ . as specified in feature1_3 and feature2_3. Both columns are excluded from predictors. The task is inferred as 'survival'. The veteran dataset is the reference data with columns trt, celltype, time, status, karno, diagtime, age, prior.

context_pointers.vague_reference: like what we did in the clinical data project last quarter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Refers to right-censored survival analysis using the veteran dataset (137 rows). The status column uses 1=event, 0=censored encoding. Unique sorted event times (status=1) yield 101 distinct timepoints as verified in feature8_survival_timepoints.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the adapter layer should be thin and separate from the actual engine logic
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Architecture constraint in start.md section 2: a thin JSON adapter reads stdin commands and calls core engine entry points, then renders structured results to stdout as key=value lines. The core engine must have no knowledge of stdin/stdout or JSON. This maps to the Separation of Concerns NFR and DIP principle.

context_pointers.vague_reference: meaningful category labels for things like bad sampling settings
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The error taxonomy defined in feature10 test cases: sample_fraction_out_of_range, sample_fraction_vector_requires_classification, sample_fraction_size_mismatch (with expected= and provided= fields), sample_fraction_sum_zero, insufficient_samples_in_class (with class=, available=, requested= fields).

Feature 5 test cases define the shapes: classification/regression are 2D (observations x trees), probability is 3D (observations x classes x trees), survival produces two 3D arrays survival_shape and chf_shape each (observations x event-times x trees). Subsetting to first K trees via num_trees_predict reduces the tree dimension.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify from the original discussion — you'll want to check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or the PRD section in start.md that covers the relevant feature. The contract is defined by the expected_output fields in those JSON test cases.

feature10_10_survival_no_covariates.json.description: A survival formula that leaves no predictor columns after consuming the time and status columns is rejected.

feature10_11_importance_not_requested.js.description: Querying variable importance on a model trained without importance scoring is rejected.

feature10_12_timepoints_non_survival.jso.description: Querying survival timepoints on a non-survival model is rejected.

feature10_13_mtry_function_error.json.description: When the predictor-count selection function raises during evaluation, training is aborted with an mtry-function error category.

feature10_1_sample_fraction_scalar_range.description: A scalar sampling fraction outside the half-open interval (0,1] is rejected with an out-of-range error category.

feature10_2_sample_fraction_vector_task..description: A per-class vector of sampling fractions is only valid for classification; supplying one for a regression response is rejected.

feature10_3_sample_fraction_vector_size..description: A per-class vector of sampling fractions must provide exactly one value per response class; a wrong-length vector is rejected with the expected and provided counts.

feature10_4_sample_fraction_vector_sum.j.description: A per-class vector of sampling fractions whose values sum to zero is rejected.

feature10_5_insufficient_samples.json.description: Sampling without replacement that requests more observations of a class than exist is rejected, reporting the class, the available count, and the requested count.

feature10_6_splitrule_task_mismatch.json.description: A splitting rule that is specific to one task family is rejected when used on data of a different family (e.g. a regression-only rule on categorical data, or survival-only rules on categorical data).

feature10_7_missing_data_training.json.description: Missing values in a predictor column used for training are rejected, naming the offending column(s); a missing value in the response is rejected with a distinct response category.

feature10_8_missing_data_prediction.json.description: Missing values in predictor columns of the data passed at prediction time are rejected, naming the offending columns in column order.

feature10_9_invalid_prediction_type.json.description: Requesting an unknown prediction output type is rejected.

feature11_dropped_unused_levels.json.description: Training on a subset that omits some response classes warns that unused response levels were dropped, naming them; predictions still span the full set of original classes (or, for probability, the surviving class count).

feature1_1_formula_classification.json.description: Resolve a formula whose response is a categorical column into a model design: the inferred learning task, the tree count actually used, and the ordered list of predictor columns (every other column).

feature1_2_formula_regression.json.description: Resolve a formula whose response is a continuous numeric column into a regression model design with the remaining columns as predictors.

feature1_3_formula_survival.json.description: Resolve a formula whose response is a right-censored time/event pair into a survival model design; the time and status columns are consumed by the response and excluded from predictors.

feature1_4_formula_probability.json.description: Resolve a formula with a categorical response while requesting class-probability estimation; the task becomes probability estimation over the response classes.

feature1_5_lhs_function_response.json.description: Accept a formula whose left-hand side wraps the response column in a user function; the design must still resolve and infer the task from the underlying response.

feature2_1_named_response_classification.description: Build a model design by naming the response column directly (instead of a formula); a categorical response yields a classification design with all other columns as predictors.

feature2_2_named_response_regression.jso.description: Name a numeric response column directly to obtain a regression design.

feature2_3_named_response_survival.json.description: Name a time column and a separate status column to obtain a survival design; both named columns are excluded from predictors.

feature3_1_matrix_classification_equival.description: Train two models with the same seed: one on a column-typed table, one on the all-numeric matrix encoding of the same data (declared as a classification task). The externally-observable label assignments must agree on every observation.

feature3_2_matrix_survival_equivalence.j.description: Train a survival model with the same seed on a typed table and on its all-numeric matrix encoding; the cumulative-hazard estimates must agree cell-for-cell.

feature3_3_matrix_predictor_selection.js.description: When the data source is an all-numeric matrix and the response is named, the predictor set is every other column of the matrix, preserving order.

feature4_reproducibility.json.description: Training twice from the same seed with identical settings produces identical predictions; the number of disagreeing observations between the two runs must be zero.

feature5_1_predict_all_classification.js.description: Per-tree prediction for a classification design returns one column per tree: an observations-by-trees matrix.

feature5_2_predict_all_regression.json.description: Per-tree prediction for a regression design returns an observations-by-trees matrix.

feature5_3_predict_all_probability.json.description: Per-tree prediction for a probability design returns a three-dimensional array of observations by classes by trees.

feature5_4_predict_all_survival.json.description: Per-tree prediction for a survival design returns survival and cumulative-hazard arrays, each observations by event-times by trees.

feature5_5_predict_subset_trees.json.description: Per-tree prediction restricted to the first K trees returns a matrix whose tree dimension equals K.

feature5_6_terminal_nodes.json.description: Requesting terminal-node identifiers returns an observations-by-trees matrix of node indices.

feature6_confusion_matrix.json.description: A classification design exposes a square confusion matrix whose rows are the true classes and whose columns are the predicted classes; each row total equals the number of training observations of that true class.

feature7_probability_outputs.json.description: Class-probability predictions form an observations-by-classes matrix whose every row sums to one with all entries in the unit interval.

feature8_survival_timepoints.json.description: A survival design reports its evaluation timepoints; these equal the sorted distinct event times observed in training.

feature9_variable_importance.json.description: When importance scoring is enabled, the design exposes one numeric importance value per predictor, keyed by predictor name.
